author: @austenwayne34
text: I have a large spring that runs through my yard. It flows at 420 gallons per minute, and there is about 14 ft of drop from where it comes out of the side of the hill to the surface of the stream. Water was bottled here from the 30's to the late 70's. It was gravity feed to the bottling house and the operation was ran off batteries before indoor electricity. There was a wheel generator that charged the extra batteries and they were swapped out when the lights started to dim. This information was passed to me by my grandfather. I bought the farm from him, and it has been a century farm in our family since 2022. I've always thought if it were possible back then, I should be able to produce enough energy to possibly offset some of my electrical costs by putting it back into the grid. I would appreciate any feedback and ideas on how and what the most efficient system would look like. Thank you all for any advice!

replies.author: @jerry23w
replies.text: That is an incredible resource you have there - many have water but not the drop in height. The poncelet wheel may be efficient in terms of the movement of a volume of water x drop in height, but the actual output would be much smaller than an overshot wheel that utilises the weight of the water over a greater time as there is more height difference between entry and exit and the poncelet relies mostly on kinetic energy which quicky disburses under load in any direction it can. You could have a serious overshot waterwheel by the sounds of it that could provide a useful amount of electricity 24/7 , although the 14 ft you have wouldnt be enough for a turbine.

replies.author: @austenwayne34
replies.text: Thank you for the advice!

author: @kadmow
text:
The forever efficiency limitation when dealing with dynamic flows.  80% of what??  Total energy (Static + Dynamic, or static head potential only?)

The Poncelet wheel - by adding the "scoop"  - and channel is adding a mini dam to the wheel - shielding the "advancing blades" /buckets until they approach an optimal impingement angle is. - many high solidity VAWTS similarly benefit if they have a n aerodynamic weathercocking "shield" added - or feathering blades.

A high % of the maximum possible is achievable - but like the Betz limit, determining limits (and what are the critical control areas/volumes) is the trick, once the entrained energy is reduced "to a point" as there is less energy in the entire flow less and less is extracted until slowdown to standstill occurs - hence we must always be careful to manage the "load" on the system. (reasons why uncontrolled, unconstrained hydrodynamic or aerodynamic rotors have a tendency to run away, or not perform to their potential (control - matching load  to power available balancing is important in many systems (controlled weirs and penstocks are simple to control as everything is constrained - run of river and wind  is more complex though "cheaper to install".

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
I like how the new copy of this wheel can account for rise and fall in the flow of the water, and, at least to some extent, be safely retracted in case of damaging flood conditions.

That said, where it was not deemed a 'navigable' water way, it would, by all means, make sense to have the wheel go entirely across the entire water way, even to the point of installing a narrowing viaduct to ensure a higher flow of water [linear speed] and a greater opportunity to interact in a parallel procession manner with improved upstream laminar flow.

Also, by holding the wheel by both ends, translational torque would be much less of an issue, as the outer end would cease to exist, eliminating the transversal moment of torque, which clearly applies strain to the pedestal, requiring more massive build to prevent damage to the superstructure.

Now, if it IS a navigable water way, one merely need raise the wheel high enough that vessels can pass under it.

On top of all of this, for flood-plane type scenarios, one can create diverter ducts around the sides, which, when the water is becoming too much, can open up to spread out the more extreme flow of water, as well as any debris that might otherwise get into and damage the wheel.

If it were made to be a large, hollow tube with a flat walkway in the middle [like a hubless wheel stretched out into a sort of tunnel] it could serve as a bridge at the same time.

And for light, one merely needs have some LED strip lights wired into the hand rails of the bridge so that it is always illuminated, and it can even be a dry place to hang out and escape a sudden rain storm [provided one did not get stranded there because of flooding, of course!]

It would actually be quite an artistic product, and, in theory, could either be a smaller wheel on arms, rotating around a walk way, OR a really LARGE wheel, fully capable of rotating all the way around the walk way.

It is advantageous for the sake of clearance to make it a wheel on arms, but it is advantageous as far as total power harvesting to make the wheel go all the way around the bridge.

I am going to draw a simple sketch of this, in case people are curious to see what I mean.

While I don't expect anybody to be taking this up as a design option, I'll be letting it out for free just in case they like it.

author: @TassieTiki
text:
Thanks Rob. There was a guy in the UK a decade or two ago who developed a "revolutionary and unique" water wheel of sorts with bold claims of 2kW from a low head stream. He was an electrician and used yoghurt cups on his test units. He was backed by a university. I can't recall his name nor can I find anything about him. The design was weird in that it wasn't actually a wheel. There was a wide top and bottom axle with a light chain on each end connecting the two. Across the breadth of the two chains was several spaced rows of his yoghurt cups. 
That's all I can remember. I wonder if you can find anything or remember it? If so, maybe a 3d printed demo of his unit would be worth considering? Just a thought . Cheers from OZ

replies.author: @TheNightwalker247
replies.text: Seems like a bucket elevator/generator. Could be useful for fall hights below 15m with space constraints for a big wheel. But it seems to me you would be better of using some kind of turbin/ turgo etc.  With the chain  you would get a lot of friction loss plus maintenance  if you run water over the chain/cups

replies.author: @jerry23w
replies.text: The device you mention was like a vertical water wheel (think of conveyor belt stood on end) that had increased efficiency because the full weight of the captured water was utilised for the entire drop. whereas the traditional round wheel is only really efficient at about the 2-4 o'clock position. Apparently there was a working prototype, but it has been scrubbed from the internet which means it was probably tooo good..... The claims of about 2kw from a small stream do sound unlikely, depending on how high a drop was incorporated. Could be a lot easier to build than a very large wheel with its infrastructure if there was say 20 + feet available and considerably cheaper!

text: Love the video! I know in a lot of places in the USA you can get in a lot of trouble if you were to install something like that boom arm water wheel,but I really like the idea, it could easily be made to automatically lower into the water if you batteries fell below a certain percentage and then lift back out when it’s at the optimal charge level. Also since it is a much more constant energy source than wind or solar you may be able to run everything off of incoming power and have a much smaller battery that just acts as a buffer.

replies.author: @sirHHO
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering  thanks for your 1000's cool videos. Have you done any video on breast shot wheels ? I'm currently working on a design of a wheel. I don't have sufficient head for an overshot and but I want to use that meter of head I got efficiency. I'm also thinking of eliminating transmission and gearbox systems by integrating the generator in within the wheel but I'm estimating something like 15-20 RPM. The flow I got is between 300-500 l/s ( seasonal changes) and the speed around 0.55 m/s. My other option is an archimrdian screw type generator as it can achieve 70-80% efficiency.

author: @chuckstoffregen8632
text: Sorry, this is off topic but I have been a subscriber for over a year and respect your knowledge base concerning 3D printers. I received an Anet A8 3D printer with no documentation, so I did some Google searching to see what I could find. I got it up and running with a couple minor setbacks but while adjusting a limit switch, I touched a couple of solder joints on the small switch pc board. I saw a bit of a spark and my controller screen showed rectagonal shapes filling the screen. I hooked up my USB cable to my laptop and the screen went to the normal view but the main board was not powered up. Whe… See more

replies.author: @natecus4926
replies.text: I had an a8 for a long time, it is very possible that something on the board is fried and you may need to get a new board. The motherboards that are specific to the a8 run about $50-60 on Amazon. However, the company bigtreetech has a skr 1.4 board that I think may work as a replacement that is only $5-10 with the stepper drivers. Hope this info helps.

replies.author: @natecus4926
replies.text: Or if you live in the US by a micro center, they sometimes have ender 3s on sale for around $100

replies.author: @TerrorTubbie666
replies.text:
Have you checked your power supply for blown fuses or other blown components?
Although I never even touched a 3Dprinter, I know enough about electronics to know 99.99% sure you don't NEED another pcb. You just need to replace the fried component(s), or most probably the fuse or the 'fuse'.
(To con-fuse you, they probably used a lowpower resistor, diode or whatever that has blown up when you made the sparks. Exactly as the engineers planned. This way it's cheap and easy to repair for experts, that will probably advice you to buy a new pcb and give them the 'old' one for free.🤔)

replies.author: @willcool713
replies.text: The combination of wind with water for a portable power solution seems natural, since streams and rivers often funnel winds along their open path. Putting an omnidirectional wind turbine in pair with a floating water wheel would work nicely, if the whole thing could be struck and re-erected without difficulty.
